name: Redspher Full-Stack Developer Interview Preparation
description: A self-contained English interview pack for the Redspher Full-Stack Developer role, covering TypeScript, React, Node.js, REST, AWS, CI/CD, business delivery and stakeholder collaboration, AI agents, MCP, automation, prototyping, real-time systems, scalability, and legacy integration.

topics.name: Runtime Validation
topics.description: Runtime validation checks real data while the application is running, because TypeScript types alone cannot prove that values from users, APIs, files, or configuration are valid.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Runtime validation checks the actual data that enters a running application. It is needed for values from users, application programming interfaces (APIs), files, and configuration because TypeScript types are removed during compilation and cannot verify those values by themselves.

## What problem does it solve?

TypeScript types disappear after compilation. An API can return a missing field, a form can send an invalid value, or an environment variable can be absent. Without a runtime check, invalid data can fail later in a less understandable place.

## How it works

A React client receives a shipment object. It checks that the id is text, the status is allowed, and the date is valid before the rest of the screen uses the object.

- Treat data that enters from another system or user as untrusted.
- Check the value with a runtime schema or explicit parser.
- Reject invalid data with a clear, safe error.
- Map valid transport data into the internal application model.

## Common mistakes

- Assuming a TypeScript interface validates JSON received from a server.
- Validating only in the browser and trusting the same data on the backend.
- Passing raw external objects through the application without mapping them.

## Key terms

- runtime schema — A description used by running code to check whether a real value has the required shape.
- boundary — A place where data enters from another component, user, file, service, or environment.
- data transfer object (DTO) — A deliberately shaped object used to move information across a system boundary.

topics.name: TypeScript Compile-Time Type Safety
topics.description: TypeScript checks how JavaScript values are used before the program runs. It catches many mismatched fields and function calls, but it cannot verify data that arrives while the application is running.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

TypeScript analyzes how JavaScript values are used before the program runs. It catches many invalid property accesses, assignments, and function calls during development, but it cannot prove that external data is valid at runtime.

## What problem does it solve?

In a large JavaScript application, one function may expect a field or value that another part never provides. Without a type check, the mistake may appear only when a user reaches that path. TypeScript finds many of these mismatches during development, but it cannot inspect real application programming interface (API) or user data by itself.

## How it works

A function accepts a ShipmentSummary with an id, status, and updatedAt value. TypeScript reports an error when a caller omits the id or passes a number as the status, before that code is deployed.

- Describe function inputs, outputs, objects, and domain values with clear types.
- Let the compiler compare how values are created, passed, and returned.
- Use type inference when the intended type is already clear from the code.
- Validate external data at runtime before treating it as a trusted typed value.

## Common mistakes

- Using any to silence a difficult error, which removes the useful safety check.
- Believing that a successful TypeScript build proves that external service or database data is valid.
- Creating complicated types that make simple business code harder to understand.

## Key terms

- static type — A description checked before the program runs.
- type inference — The compiler determines a value's type from how the value is created.
- compile-time error — A problem reported while the code is being checked or built.

topics.name: React Rendering Model
topics.description: React rendering turns component state, props, and context into user-interface output. A component runs again when one of these inputs changes, and React then updates the necessary browser elements.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

React rendering runs component functions to calculate the user interface for the current inputs. Those inputs include state, values supplied by a parent through props, and shared context; React then compares the new output with the previous output before updating the browser.

## What problem does it solve?

A developer may assume that rendering means rewriting the entire page or that changing any variable updates the screen. This leads to incorrect state placement, unnecessary effects, and confusing bugs when a component displays old or unexpected values.

## How it works

A shipment row receives a status prop. When the parent passes a new status, the row component runs again and returns new output. React compares that output with the previous one and changes only the affected browser element.

- State, props, or context changes schedule a new render for affected components.
- Each component calculates output from its current inputs.
- React compares the new output with the previous output.
- React applies the required browser changes after rendering finishes.

## Common mistakes

- Changing a normal variable and expecting React to render again.
- Calling side-effecting code while the component is rendering.
- Assuming every parent render must recreate every browser element.

## Key terms

- render — Running a component to calculate its current user-interface output.
- reconciliation — React compares previous and new output to determine which browser changes are needed.
- commit — The phase in which React applies calculated changes to the browser.

topics.name: React State Ownership
topics.description: React state ownership decides which component or data layer controls a changing value and which parts of the application may update or read it.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

React state ownership defines which component or data layer controls a changing value and which code may update it. A value should normally live in the smallest owner that can share it with every component that genuinely needs it.

## What problem does it solve?

When every value is placed in a global store, unrelated features become coupled and updates are harder to follow. When shared state is kept too locally, components duplicate data or use complicated communication paths. Remote server data also behaves differently from temporary interface state.

## How it works

An open modal remains local to its screen. A selected warehouse is lifted to the nearest parent that coordinates two children. Shipment data is stored in a query cache because the server owns it and controls its freshness.

- Identify who owns the value and which components need to read or change it.
- Start locally and lift state only to the nearest common owner.
- Treat remote data as server state with fetching, caching, and invalidation rules.
- Use context or a global store only for genuinely broad client-side concerns.

## Common mistakes

- Putting all application data into one global store by default.
- Copying server data into several local or global stores that become inconsistent.
- Using context for high-frequency values that rerender a large tree.

## Key terms

- local state — State owned by one component or a small part of the component tree.
- server state — Remote data whose source of truth is a backend system.
- query cache — Client storage that manages remote data freshness and refetching.

topics.name: React Effects
topics.description: A React effect synchronizes a component with something outside React, such as a browser API, subscription, timer, or external service.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

A React effect runs after rendering to synchronize a component with something outside React. Examples include browser APIs, timers, subscriptions, network connections, and third-party widgets.

## What problem does it solve?

Effects are easy to misuse as a general place for calculations or event logic. This creates duplicate state, dependency problems, repeated requests, and race conditions. Values that can be calculated during rendering usually do not need an effect.

## How it works

A shipment screen subscribes to browser notifications when a shipment id changes. The effect starts the subscription and returns a cleanup function, so the old subscription is removed before a new one is created.

- Use an effect only when rendering must synchronize with an external system.
- Include every reactive value that the effect reads in its dependency list.
- Return cleanup code for subscriptions, timers, and other ongoing work.
- Cancel or ignore stale asynchronous work when dependencies change.

## Common mistakes

- Using an effect to calculate a value that could be derived during rendering.
- Suppressing dependency warnings instead of fixing the data flow.
- Starting asynchronous work without cleanup or stale-result protection.

## Key terms

- effect — Work that synchronizes a rendered component with something outside React.
- dependency — A reactive value whose change requires the effect to run again.
- cleanup — Code that stops or reverses the previous effect before replacement or removal.

topics.name: Asynchronous UI States
topics.description: Asynchronous UI states describe what the user sees while remote data is loading, empty, refreshing, being changed, successful, or failed.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Asynchronous user-interface (UI) state describes what the user sees while remote work is in progress or its result is uncertain. Useful interfaces distinguish initial loading, empty data, background refresh, a pending change, success, and failure instead of reducing everything to one loading flag.

## What problem does it solve?

Networks can be slow, fail, or return no data. A write can time out after the server accepted it. If the interface ignores these possibilities, users submit twice, lose useful data, or cannot tell whether they should wait, retry, or correct an input.

## How it works

A shipment list keeps its current rows visible during a background refresh. A failed update shows which field is invalid. A timeout marks the operation as uncertain and checks the server before allowing another submission.

- Model initial loading, empty data, background refresh, submission, success, and failure separately.
- Keep useful existing data visible while non-destructive refresh work runs.
- Give recoverable errors a clear retry or correction action.
- Represent uncertain write outcomes honestly and reconcile them with the server.

## Common mistakes

- Using one loading boolean for every request state.
- Replacing useful data with a full-screen spinner during every refresh.
- Allowing repeated submission when the first request may already have succeeded.

## Key terms

- empty state — The interface shown when a successful request contains no items.
- background refresh — Updating remote data while keeping the current result usable.
- reconciliation — Checking the server and bringing the interface back to the true state.

topics.name: Frontend Feature Architecture
topics.description: Frontend feature architecture organizes screens, components, data access, and business behavior around clear feature boundaries.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Frontend feature architecture organizes user-interface screens, components, data access, and business behavior around clear product capabilities. Each feature exposes a small intentional interface so that other parts of the application do not depend on its internal details.

## What problem does it solve?

A growing React application becomes hard to change when files are grouped only by technical type or when every feature imports internal files from every other feature. Shared folders also become dumping grounds that hide ownership.

## How it works

A shipment-tracking feature owns its screens, hooks, API adapter, validation, and tests. Other features import only its public entry point, while generic buttons and form controls live in a small shared UI library.

- Group feature-specific screens, components, hooks, tests, and data access together.
- Expose a small public entry point and prevent deep imports into feature internals.
- Keep business rules with the feature that owns them.
- Move code to a shared area only after it is stable and genuinely generic.

## Common mistakes

- Creating one global components folder for every reusable-looking file.
- Allowing features to import each other's internal implementation details.
- Putting business rules inside generic visual components.

## Key terms

- feature boundary — A clear ownership line around one business capability.
- public interface — The small set of exports that other parts of the application may use.
- deep import — Importing an internal file instead of using a module's public entry point.

topics.name: JavaScript Event Loop
topics.description: The JavaScript event loop decides when synchronous code, promise callbacks, timers, and other queued work may run on the main JavaScript thread.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

JavaScript normally runs one piece of synchronous code at a time on a call stack. The event loop selects queued work when that stack becomes empty, while promise callbacks and timer callbacks follow different queueing rules.

## What problem does it solve?

Developers can assume asynchronous callbacks run immediately or in the order they were written. Long synchronous work can also block unrelated browser updates or server requests because queued work cannot run until the current stack finishes.

## How it works

A function schedules Promise.then and setTimeout callbacks. After the current synchronous code ends, the promise callback runs as a microtask before the timer task. A long calculation before that point delays both callbacks.

- Synchronous code runs on the current call stack until it finishes.
- Promise callbacks wait in the microtask queue.
- Timers and similar callbacks wait as later tasks.
- When the stack is empty, microtasks run before the next task.

## Common mistakes

- Believing setTimeout with zero delay runs immediately.
- Running processor-intensive synchronous work on a request or rendering path.
- Ignoring rejected promises because asynchronous errors do not behave like ordinary return values.

## Key terms

- call stack — The current chain of JavaScript functions being executed.
- microtask — High-priority queued work such as a resolved promise callback.
- task — A later event-loop item such as a timer or browser event.

topics.name: Node.js Service Structure
topics.description: A well-structured Node.js service separates HTTP handling, business rules, and infrastructure so that each part can change and be tested without rewriting the others.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

A maintainable Node.js service gives web-request handling, business decisions, and infrastructure integrations separate responsibilities. The boundaries are not extra layers for their own sake; they make important behavior easier to test, replace, and reason about.

## What problem does it solve?

When routes contain validation, queries, rules, logging, and partner calls in one function, every change becomes risky. Tests need many mocks, framework details spread through the code, and failures are difficult to trace.

## How it works

A create-order route validates the request and calls a use case. The use case applies booking rules through a repository interface, while a database adapter implements that interface.

- Keep route handlers focused on the transport protocol.
- Put the business workflow in application or domain functions.
- Access databases and external systems through narrow adapters.
- Map errors and add logs at clear boundaries.

## Common mistakes

- Putting business rules directly inside Express routes.
- Creating many layers that only forward the same arguments.
- Hiding all failures inside one generic error type.

## Key terms

- controller — Code that receives an HTTP request and turns the result into an HTTP response.
- use case — A function or service that performs one business workflow.
- adapter — Code that connects application logic to a database, API, queue, or framework.

topics.name: Resilient Dependency Calls
topics.description: Resilient dependency calls prevent a slow or failing database, API, or service from consuming all of an application's time and capacity.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

A resilient dependency call limits how much time and capacity an external database or service may consume. Timeouts, controlled retries, concurrency limits, and fallback decisions prevent one slow dependency from exhausting the entire application.

## What problem does it solve?

Without boundaries, requests wait indefinitely, retries multiply traffic, and one failing partner can exhaust connection pools or workers. The failure then spreads to unrelated features that use the same service.

## How it works

A transport-partner call has a short timeout. Only temporary network failures are retried with delayed attempts. A circuit breaker pauses new calls after repeated failures, while a concurrency limit protects the remaining request capacity.

- Give every dependency call an explicit timeout or deadline.
- Retry only temporary failures when the operation is safe to repeat.
- Limit concurrent work so one dependency cannot consume all capacity.
- Stop repeated calls temporarily when failures show the dependency is unhealthy.

## Common mistakes

- Retrying every failure immediately and without a limit.
- Using a timeout that is longer than the user's entire request deadline.
- Treating a circuit breaker as a replacement for normal error handling.

## Key terms

- timeout — A maximum amount of time allowed for an operation.
- backoff — Waiting longer between repeated retry attempts.
- circuit breaker — A control that temporarily stops calls after repeated failures.
- bulkhead — Resource isolation that prevents one workload from consuming everything.

topics.name: REST API Design
topics.description: REST API design creates predictable HTTP contracts for reading and changing business resources while allowing clients and servers to evolve safely.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

REST is an application programming interface (API) style that exposes business resources through predictable web requests and responses. Consistent URLs, methods, status codes, data shapes, and error formats create a contract that clients can understand and servers can evolve safely.

## What problem does it solve?

Inconsistent APIs force each client to learn different naming, error shapes, and pagination rules. Unbounded collection endpoints become slow, while breaking response changes can stop deployed frontends or partner systems.

## How it works

A shipments endpoint accepts documented filters and a page cursor. Invalid input returns the same structured error format as other endpoints. A new optional field is added without removing fields that existing clients still use.

- Define resource names, methods, status codes, and response shapes consistently.
- Validate requests and describe the contract with a machine-readable schema such as OpenAPI.
- Bound collections with filtering, sorting, pagination, and maximum limits.
- Prefer additive changes and deprecate breaking behavior through a migration period.

## Common mistakes

- Returning different error formats from different endpoints.
- Exposing raw database rows as the public API contract.
- Adding offset pagination without considering large or rapidly changing collections.
- Removing a field without measuring whether clients still use it.

## Key terms

- resource — A business object or collection exposed through an API.
- contract — The documented request and response behavior clients may rely on.
- cursor — A stable position used to request the next part of a collection.
- backward compatibility — Allowing existing clients to keep working after a change.

topics.name: API Authentication
topics.description: API authentication proves which user, service, or session is making a request. It establishes identity before the application considers what that caller may do.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Application programming interface (API) authentication verifies evidence that identifies the user, service, or session making a protected request. It establishes who the caller is before the application makes a separate authorization decision about what that caller may do.

## What problem does it solve?

Without authentication, an API cannot reliably connect a request to a user or service. A copied, expired, incorrectly signed, or incorrectly issued token may be accepted if the API only checks that some credential is present.

## How it works

An employee signs in through a trusted identity provider and receives a token. The Node.js API verifies the token's signature, issuer, audience, and expiry before treating the request as belonging to that employee.

- A trusted identity system verifies the user or service and creates a session or token.
- The client sends the credential with each protected request.
- The API validates the credential and extracts the authenticated identity.
- Expired, invalid, or incorrectly issued credentials are rejected.

## Common mistakes

- Checking only that a token exists without validating its signature, issuer, audience, and expiry.
- Storing a long-lived bearer token where injected browser code can easily read it.
- Treating authentication as proof that the caller may perform every action.

## Key terms

- identity provider — A trusted system that verifies identity and issues a session or token.
- access token — A credential presented to an API as evidence of an authenticated session.
- claim — A statement inside a token about its issuer, audience, user, session, or expiry.

topics.name: Idempotent Write Operations
topics.description: An idempotent write can be repeated after a timeout without creating a second business effect such as another order, payment, or notification.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

An idempotent operation has the same business effect when the same logical request is repeated. This is especially important after timeouts, because a client may retry without knowing whether the first attempt already succeeded.

## What problem does it solve?

Networks can lose responses even when the server completed the request. A client then retries because it cannot know the outcome. Without idempotency, a safe-looking retry may create duplicate orders or other expensive side effects.

## How it works

A client creates a transport order with an idempotency key. The server atomically reserves the key and stores the completed response. A retry with the same key returns the first result instead of creating another order.

- The client reuses one stable key for retries of the same logical operation.
- The server reserves that key atomically so only one request can win.
- The completed result is stored and returned for matching retries.
- A reused key with different input is rejected, and old keys expire deliberately.

## Common mistakes

- Generating a new idempotency key for every retry.
- Checking for an existing key and creating the record in two non-atomic steps.
- Assuming every web-request method is automatically safe to retry.

## Key terms

- idempotency key — A client-provided identifier for one logical operation.
- atomic operation — A change that completes as one indivisible step.
- response replay — Returning the stored result of the first completed request.

topics.name: Database Concurrency Control
topics.description: Database concurrency control keeps shared data correct when several requests try to read and change the same business value at nearly the same time.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Database concurrency control keeps shared business rules true when several requests read or change the same data at nearly the same time. It uses database guarantees or explicit version checks to prevent valid individual operations from producing an invalid combined result.

## What problem does it solve?

Application code may read that capacity is available in two requests before either one writes. If both updates then succeed, the system can oversell capacity or silently overwrite a newer change.

## How it works

Two users reserve the last transport slot. An update succeeds only when the stored version still matches the version each user read. One request wins, while the other receives a conflict and reloads the current state.

- State the invariant that must remain true, such as capacity never becoming negative.
- Choose a database-enforced constraint, conditional update, version check, or lock.
- Keep the transaction small and avoid waiting for remote services inside it.
- Return a clear conflict or retry path for the request that loses.

## Common mistakes

- Reading first and writing later without an atomic condition.
- Holding a database lock while waiting for a slow partner service.
- Silently overwriting a newer value after a concurrency conflict.

## Key terms

- invariant — A rule that must remain true for valid data.
- optimistic locking — Rejecting an update when the record changed after it was read.
- transaction — A group of database changes that succeeds or fails as one unit.

topics.name: SQL Query Performance
topics.description: SQL query performance is the practice of measuring how a database executes a query and reducing the work required to return the needed result.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Structured Query Language (SQL) performance is the practice of measuring how a relational database executes a query and reducing the work required for the needed result. The goal is to fix the proven bottleneck rather than guessing that every slow endpoint needs another index.

## What problem does it solve?

As a table grows, a query that once seemed fast may scan many rows, sort large results, or use an inefficient join. Adding random indexes can increase storage and write cost without helping the actual query.

## How it works

A shipment search is traced with its real filters. The database's EXPLAIN command shows a full scan and sort. A composite index matching the filter and order reduces examined rows, and a load test confirms the improvement.

- Capture the exact query, parameters, timing, and realistic data volume.
- Inspect the execution plan for scans, joins, sorts, and index use.
- Change the query shape or add a focused index that supports the access pattern.
- Measure the new read speed and the added write, storage, and maintenance cost.

## Common mistakes

- Adding indexes before checking the execution plan.
- Testing only with a small development database.
- Selecting every column when the endpoint needs only a few.
- Ignoring pagination and returning an unbounded result.

## Key terms

- execution plan — The database's description of how it will run a query.
- index — An additional data structure that helps find rows without scanning all of them.
- composite index — An index containing more than one column in a defined order.

topics.name: Caching and Freshness
topics.description: Caching stores a reusable copy of data to reduce repeated work, while freshness rules decide how long that copy may safely differ from the source.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

A cache stores a reusable copy of data so an application can avoid repeating expensive work. Every cached value also needs a freshness policy that defines when the copy may be reused, refreshed, invalidated, or temporarily served while stale.

## What problem does it solve?

A cache can make a system faster while also hiding important changes. A public tracking summary may tolerate a short delay, but an old final price or booking state can mislead a user and cause an incorrect decision.

## How it works

A shipment summary is cached for thirty seconds and invalidated when a status event arrives. Final price quotes are not reused after their validity time. Metrics record hit rate and the age of served values.

- Decide which data may be cached and how stale it may become.
- Choose clear cache keys, ownership, and an expiry or invalidation rule.
- Define behavior for misses, outages, and many requests rebuilding one value.
- Measure value age and correctness as well as cache hit rate.

## Common mistakes

- Caching every frequently read value without considering business risk.
- Using a long time-to-live value as the only invalidation strategy for critical data.
- Allowing many requests to rebuild the same expired value at once.

## Key terms

- time to live (TTL) — The period after which a cached value expires.
- invalidation — Removing or replacing a cached value when the source changes.
- cache stampede — Many requests trying to rebuild the same missing value at once.

topics.name: Real-Time Update Delivery
topics.description: Real-time update delivery chooses how a server communicates changing information to a browser through polling, Server-Sent Events, or WebSocket.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Real-time delivery sends changing information to a client without waiting for a normal page reload. Polling, Server-Sent Events, and WebSocket support different update frequencies and communication directions, so the transport should match the actual workflow.

## What problem does it solve?

Polling is simple but can waste requests or add delay. Persistent connections reduce delay but require connection management and reconnect handling. The most complex transport is not automatically the best choice.

## How it works

A read-only tracking screen receives one-way status changes every few seconds. It uses Server-Sent Events for updates and a REST endpoint for the current snapshot. After reconnecting, the client reloads missed state.

- Clarify acceptable delay, update frequency, direction, and expected connection count.
- Use polling for simple or infrequent updates when extra delay is acceptable.
- Use SSE for one-way server push and WebSocket for true two-way communication.
- Keep a snapshot and resynchronization path for reconnecting clients.

## Common mistakes

- Choosing WebSocket only because the feature is described as real time.
- Assuming a persistent connection never disconnects.
- Sending events without a way to recover missed or duplicated updates.

## Key terms

- polling — Repeatedly asking the server whether data changed.
- Server-Sent Events — A persistent HTTP stream that sends events from server to browser.
- WebSocket — A persistent connection that supports messages in both directions.

topics.name: AWS Application Architecture
topics.description: AWS application architecture combines managed hosting, compute, data, networking, security, observability, and deployment services into an operable application.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

AWS application architecture combines Amazon Web Services for hosting, compute, data, networking, security, monitoring, and deployment. A good design uses the smallest practical set of services that meets the application's traffic, reliability, security, and operational needs.

## What problem does it solve?

Cloud services reduce infrastructure work but do not remove design responsibility. A public database, an overly broad Identity and Access Management (IAM) role, missing alarms, or a compute service that does not fit the workload can still make the application insecure or unreliable.

## How it works

A React build is stored in Amazon S3 object storage and delivered through the CloudFront content delivery network. A Node.js API runs on suitable managed compute, reaches a private managed database, reads secrets through an IAM role, and sends logs and metrics to CloudWatch.

- Separate static frontend delivery, backend compute, and managed data services.
- Use least-privilege IAM roles and managed secret storage.
- Control network access and encrypt traffic and stored data.
- Automate deployment, monitoring, backup, health checks, and rollback.

## Common mistakes

- Giving application workloads administrator permissions.
- Storing database credentials in source code or a container image.
- Assuming a managed service requires no backup, alarm, or recovery plan.
- Selecting services independently without tracing the full request path.

## Key terms

- IAM role — Temporary permissions that an AWS workload may assume.
- Amazon S3 — AWS object storage commonly used for static files and frontend builds.
- CloudFront — An AWS content delivery network that serves files from locations closer to users.
- CloudWatch — AWS tools for logs, metrics, and alarms.

topics.name: Choosing AWS Compute
topics.description: Choosing AWS compute means selecting Lambda, containers, or another runtime according to traffic, execution behavior, operational needs, and cost.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Choosing AWS compute means deciding how application code should run on Amazon Web Services. Functions run by AWS Lambda, containers run by Elastic Container Service (ECS), and other runtimes fit different traffic patterns, execution times, operational needs, and cost models.

## What problem does it solve?

A poor compute choice can create unnecessary cost, slow startup, difficult scaling, or limits that do not fit the application. A short event-driven task and a continuously running service have different needs.

## How it works

A file-processing job runs only when a document arrives, so Lambda can execute it and stop. A Node.js service with long-lived connections runs in ECS containers because it needs a continuous process.

- Identify whether work is short, continuous, event-driven, or connection-heavy.
- Compare runtime limits, startup time, scaling, and networking needs.
- Estimate request cost, idle cost, and operational effort.
- Choose the simplest service that meets reliability needs.

## Common mistakes

- Choosing Lambda for every workload without considering long-running connections.
- Choosing containers without considering idle capacity and maintenance.
- Comparing only infrastructure price and ignoring engineering effort.

## Key terms

- Lambda — A managed service that runs a function in response to events.
- ECS — An AWS service for running and managing containers.
- cold start — Startup delay when a new function environment must be created.

topics.name: Full-Stack Testing Strategy
topics.description: A full-stack testing strategy places different tests around business rules, technical boundaries, service contracts, and critical user journeys.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

A full-stack testing strategy places different kinds of tests around business rules, technical boundaries, service contracts, and critical user journeys. It chooses the cheapest reliable test for each risk instead of forcing every behavior through a slow end-to-end test.

## What problem does it solve?

A suite made mostly of browser tests is slow and fragile, while a suite made only of isolated unit tests may miss database, schema, or integration failures. The goal is fast feedback plus confidence at real boundaries.

## How it works

Pricing rules and status transitions use unit tests. The Node.js API is tested with a real test database. A contract check protects the React client, and a small browser test covers the complete create-order journey.

- Test deterministic business rules with fast focused tests.
- Use integration tests for HTTP, database, queue, and external adapter boundaries.
- Check the frontend and backend against the same API contract.
- Keep end-to-end tests for a small set of critical user journeys.

## Common mistakes

- Mocking every dependency so integration behavior is never tested.
- Using end-to-end tests for simple rules that a unit test could cover.
- Keeping flaky tests without investigating why their result changes.
- Asserting implementation details instead of observable behavior.

## Key terms

- unit test — A fast test of a small piece of logic.
- integration test — A test that exercises a real boundary between components or technologies.
- contract test — A test that checks whether a service and its consumer agree on data behavior.
- end-to-end test — A test of a complete user journey through the running system.

topics.name: Safe CI/CD Delivery
topics.description: Safe CI/CD delivery turns a source change into one tested version that can move through environments, be activated gradually, observed, and rolled back.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Continuous integration and continuous delivery, usually shortened to CI/CD, turn a source change into a tested and traceable release. A safe pipeline promotes the same version through environments, controls rollout, observes production behavior, and keeps a practical recovery path.

## What problem does it solve?

Rebuilding separately in each environment creates unverified differences. Frontend, backend, and database versions may overlap during deployment, so an incompatible change or irreversible migration can break users before a rollback is possible.

## How it works

The pipeline builds one versioned backend image and frontend bundle. The backend first accepts both old and new clients. A feature flag activates the new flow for a small group, while smoke tests and error metrics decide whether rollout continues.

- Run repeatable quality, test, build, and security checks.
- Build once and promote the same immutable version through environments.
- Deploy backward-compatible application and database changes before dependent clients.
- Separate deployment from activation and keep a tested rollback path.

## Common mistakes

- Rebuilding different artifacts for staging and production.
- Deploying a frontend that requires an API change before the backend supports it.
- Using an irreversible database migration in the same step as application activation.
- Treating a successful pipeline as proof that the feature works for users.

## Key terms

- immutable artifact — A versioned build that is not changed after creation.
- feature flag — A switch that controls whether deployed behavior is active.
- canary rollout — Enabling a release for a small portion of traffic first.
- expand-migrate-contract — A sequence that keeps old and new versions compatible during a schema change.

topics.name: Production Incident Response
topics.description: Production incident response reduces active user impact, finds the failing part from evidence, verifies recovery, and prevents the same failure from returning.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Production incident response is the coordinated work of reducing active user impact and restoring a service safely. After stabilization, the team uses timelines, logs, metrics, and traces to find the cause, verify recovery, and prevent recurrence.

## What problem does it solve?

During an outage, random code changes can increase damage and destroy evidence. Logs without request context, metrics without a timeline, or no rollback plan make it difficult to understand whether a release, dependency, database, or traffic change caused the problem.

## How it works

After an order release, latency and errors rise. The team disables the feature, identifies affected requests, follows correlation identifiers (IDs) through traces, finds a slow database query, verifies recovery, and adds a targeted alert and regression test.

- Reduce impact with rollback, a feature switch, traffic control, or another safe mitigation.
- Define the time window, affected users, and symptoms before forming a cause.
- Correlate logs, metrics, traces, dependency behavior, and recent changes.
- Verify recovery with the same signals and record preventive follow-up work.

## Common mistakes

- Continuing deep diagnosis while a safe rollback could immediately protect users.
- Searching logs without narrowing the affected time and request path.
- Calling the first visible error the root cause without testing the hypothesis.
- Closing the incident without an alert, test, or operational improvement.

## Key terms

- correlation ID — An identifier used to follow one request across logs and services.
- mitigation — A quick safe action that reduces impact before the full cause is fixed.
- root cause — The underlying condition that allowed the incident to occur.

topics.name: Requirements Clarification
topics.description: Requirements clarification turns a vague request into a shared description of the user problem, expected outcome, rules, constraints, and evidence of success.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Requirements clarification turns a vague request into a shared description of the user problem, expected outcome, rules, constraints, and evidence of success. It separates the real need from a stakeholder's first suggested implementation.

## What problem does it solve?

Stakeholders often describe a preferred feature, such as adding artificial intelligence (AI), without explaining which decision is slow or which user is affected. Starting implementation immediately can produce a technically impressive feature that changes no useful outcome.

## How it works

A stakeholder asks for an AI-based feature to reduce delays. The team maps the current exception workflow, finds that operators spend time gathering data, and defines success as faster case preparation with no automatic business action.

- Identify the user, current workflow, pain point, and desired business outcome.
- Make business rules, permissions, data, exceptions, and operational ownership explicit.
- Agree on measurable success and failures that are not acceptable.
- Propose a small first slice that tests the most important assumption.

## Common mistakes

- Accepting a requested technology as the problem definition.
- Discussing architecture before understanding who uses the result.
- Leaving success vague so the delivered feature cannot be evaluated.
- Trying to solve every future case in the first release.

## Key terms

- business outcome — The measurable change the organization wants to achieve.
- acceptance criterion — A clear condition that describes correct feature behavior.
- constraint — A limit the solution must respect, such as permission, time, or cost.

topics.name: End-to-End Feature Delivery
topics.description: End-to-end feature delivery carries one business capability from a clarified need through frontend, backend, data, testing, release, and outcome measurement.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

End-to-end feature delivery carries one business capability from a clarified user need through implementation, testing, release, and outcome measurement. Ownership includes coordinating the frontend, backend, data, and operational work needed to make the result useful in production.

## What problem does it solve?

A feature can be complete in one code repository but still fail because API contracts, permissions, data changes, support behavior, or rollout were not coordinated. Technology lists do not explain whether the engineering decisions solved the actual problem.

## How it works

A transport-order feature is split into a small vertical slice. The team agrees on the API contract, builds the React flow and Node.js rules, tests the boundaries, releases behind a flag, and measures completion rate and errors.

- Turn the clarified need into a small vertical slice with acceptance criteria.
- Coordinate user experience, API contracts, business rules, data, and permissions.
- Test the highest-risk rules and boundaries before release.
- Roll out safely and compare technical and business signals with the original goal.

## Common mistakes

- Treating frontend and backend work as independent tickets without one shared behavior.
- Listing technologies without explaining decisions and trade-offs.
- Calling deployment complete without checking production or business results.
- Delivering a large first version that cannot be evaluated incrementally.

## Key terms

- vertical slice — A small useful feature delivered through every required application layer.
- trade-off — A choice that improves one quality while accepting a cost in another.
- rollout — The controlled process of enabling a released feature for users.

topics.name: LLM Application Integration
topics.description: LLM application integration adds language-model capabilities while deterministic application code keeps control of data access, validation, permissions, and business actions.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

A large language model, or LLM, can generate or interpret language inside an application, but its output is uncertain rather than guaranteed truth. A safe integration keeps permissions, data access, validation, and business actions under deterministic application control.

## What problem does it solve?

A model can produce useful summaries, but it can also invent facts, ignore instructions, expose sensitive data, or return an invalid action. Directly trusting its text can damage data or mislead users.

## How it works

A support assistant reads authorized shipment information and drafts a customer update. Normal code checks permissions, allowed values, and the message type before a person approves sending it.

- Give the model a narrow task and only the context it needs.
- Request structured output when code must process the answer.
- Validate the result with schemas, permissions, and business rules.
- Measure quality, latency, cost, and failure cases.

## Common mistakes

- Treating confident model text as verified information.
- Sending more private data than the task requires.
- Allowing free-form output to change business records directly.

## Key terms

- hallucination — A model answer that sounds plausible but is unsupported or wrong.
- grounding — Giving the model trusted source information that should support its answer.
- structured output — A response constrained to a known data shape that code can validate.

topics.name: Tool-Using AI Agents
topics.description: A tool-using AI agent can choose and call approved application functions across several steps while an orchestrator limits its permission, time, cost, and authority.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

A tool-using artificial-intelligence (AI) agent can choose approved application functions, inspect their results, and continue through several steps toward a goal. An orchestrator still limits the agent's permissions, time, cost, retries, and authority over real business actions.

## What problem does it solve?

A plain model can only return text. Business workflows need current data or actions, but broad access can cause repeated calls, high cost, unauthorized changes, or effects that are difficult to reverse.

## How it works

An agent handling a delayed shipment reads status and policy data through restricted tools. It proposes options, a person approves one, and a separate write tool performs the selected action.

- Expose small tools with clear inputs, outputs, and permissions.
- Track the goal, state, results, and remaining limits.
- Validate every call and require approval for high-impact actions.
- Record the execution so decisions can be reviewed.

## Common mistakes

- Giving the agent one broad tool for arbitrary database operations.
- Allowing unlimited steps, retries, or repeated tool calls.
- Relying on the model itself to enforce authorization.

## Key terms

- agent — Software that uses a model to select and perform multiple steps toward a goal.
- tool — A defined application function that the agent is allowed to request.
- orchestrator — The component that manages agent state, limits, calls, and results.

topics.name: Model Context Protocol Integration
topics.description: MCP integration gives AI applications a standard way to discover and call tools or read context exposed by separate servers, while normal security controls still protect every capability.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

The Model Context Protocol, or MCP, is a standard way for AI applications to discover tools and read context exposed by separate servers. It standardizes connectivity, but each server must still enforce normal authentication, authorization, validation, and auditing.

## What problem does it solve?

Without a shared protocol, each AI client may need a custom connector for every internal system. This repeats integration work, but standard connectivity still does not replace authentication, authorization, or validation.

## How it works

A logistics MCP server exposes a typed shipment lookup tool. Approved AI clients can discover it, while the server checks caller identity and shipment permission before returning data.

- An MCP server publishes defined tools or context capabilities.
- An MCP client discovers those capabilities for the AI host.
- The host requests a capability when it can help the task.
- The server validates identity, arguments, permission, and result data.

## Common mistakes

- Assuming MCP automatically provides authorization for every tool.
- Exposing broad internal APIs instead of narrow capabilities.
- Using MCP where one ordinary API integration would be simpler.

## Key terms

- MCP host — The application that provides the AI experience and manages model interaction.
- MCP client — The protocol component that connects a host to an MCP server.
- MCP server — A service that exposes tools or context through the protocol.

topics.name: Legacy System Integration
topics.description: Legacy system integration connects new software to an important older application through a controlled boundary while preserving behavior and reducing migration risk.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Legacy integration connects new software to an older system that still carries important business behavior or data. A stable boundary protects new code from undocumented details while the team learns the old system and reduces migration risk incrementally.

## What problem does it solve?

An old application often contains undocumented business rules and direct database assumptions. A big rewrite can miss those rules, while new services that read the legacy database directly spread the same coupling into the modern system.

## How it works

A Node.js service needs data from a PHP application. The team studies logs and real workflows, adds characterization tests, and exposes a small facade API that translates legacy data into the new domain model.

- Discover actual behavior, data ownership, dependencies, and hidden business rules.
- Protect important existing behavior with characterization and contract tests.
- Connect through a stable API, facade, or anti-corruption adapter.
- Migrate one bounded capability at a time with monitoring and rollback.

## Common mistakes

- Starting a full rewrite before understanding production behavior.
- Allowing several new services to read and write the legacy database directly.
- Changing behavior without characterization tests or a comparison baseline.
- Keeping old and new paths forever without a decommission plan.

## Key terms

- characterization test — A test that records what an existing system currently does.
- facade — A small stable interface placed in front of a more complicated system.
- anti-corruption layer — Code that prevents legacy concepts from spreading into a new design.
- strangler pattern — Replacing one bounded legacy capability at a time.

topics.name: Type Narrowing with unknown
topics.description: Type narrowing proves what an uncertain value is before the code uses it. The unknown type preserves safety because the program must perform a real check first.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Type narrowing uses runtime checks to prove that an uncertain TypeScript value has a more specific type. The unknown type is safer than any because code must inspect the value before reading properties or passing it to typed functions.

## What problem does it solve?

Values from caught errors, parsed JSON, and loosely typed libraries may not have the form the code expects. Using any allows every operation without proof, so an invalid property access can fail at runtime. Unknown forces the developer to check the value before reading or calling it.

## How it works

A catch block receives an unknown error. The code first checks whether it is an Error object. It uses error.message when the check succeeds and a generic safe message for every other value.

- Represent an uncertain value as unknown instead of any.
- Check it with typeof, instanceof, a property guard, or a runtime schema.
- Use the value only inside the branch where the check proves its type.
- Provide a controlled fallback when no supported type matches.

## Common mistakes

- Casting an unknown value to the desired type without checking it.
- Using any because narrowing requires a few additional lines of code.
- Writing a type guard that checks only one field but assumes the whole object is valid.

## Key terms

- unknown — A type for a value that cannot be used until the code checks what it is.
- type guard — A runtime check that proves a value has a more specific TypeScript type.
- type assertion — A developer instruction that tells TypeScript to trust a type without adding a runtime check.

topics.name: Discriminated Unions for Domain States
topics.description: A discriminated union represents each allowed business state as a separate object shape. It makes impossible combinations harder to create and missing cases easier to detect.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

A discriminated union represents each valid business state as a separate object shape with one shared field that identifies the state. This makes impossible field combinations harder to create and helps the compiler detect missing cases.

## What problem does it solve?

One object with many optional fields can represent combinations that make no business sense. A delivered shipment may accidentally contain cancellation details, or code may forget to handle a new delayed state. Separate state variants make the valid possibilities visible to the compiler and the reader.

## How it works

A shipment is a union of planned, dispatched, delayed, delivered, and cancelled objects. Each object has a status field and only the fields needed for that state. A switch on status handles every variant.

- Create one object type for each valid business state.
- Give every variant the same discriminant field, such as status.
- Use the discriminant to narrow the union before reading state-specific fields.
- Use exhaustive handling so a newly added state creates a visible compiler error.

## Common mistakes

- Using one large object with many optional fields for unrelated states.
- Allowing status transitions to be changed from many unrelated components.
- Assuming persisted status text is valid without runtime validation.

## Key terms

- discriminated union — A union of object types identified by one shared field.
- discriminant — The shared field, such as status, that identifies the current variant.
- exhaustive check — A check that makes missing union variants visible during development.

topics.name: Safe Git Collaboration
topics.description: Safe Git collaboration keeps changes small, reviewable, and recoverable while several developers work on the same codebase. It uses clear commits and deliberate integration instead of treating Git as only a backup tool.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Safe Git collaboration records changes in focused commits and integrates them through a reviewable history. Clear branches, pull requests, conflict resolution, and recovery commands help several developers change the same codebase without treating Git as only a backup tool.

## What problem does it solve?

Large mixed commits hide why code changed and make review or rollback difficult. Long-lived branches drift away from the main branch, and careless conflict resolution can silently remove another developer's work. A shared workflow reduces these risks before the continuous-integration (CI) pipeline begins.

## How it works

A developer builds one order-validation change in several focused commits. Before opening the pull request, the branch is updated from main, conflicts are resolved with both authors' intent in mind, and the final diff and tests are reviewed.

- Create small commits that each describe one understandable change.
- Update the branch regularly so conflicts stay small and visible.
- Use a pull request to review the diff, tests, and design decisions.
- Merge or rebase according to the team policy and use revert when a shared change must be undone.

## Common mistakes

- Putting formatting, refactoring, and business changes into one large commit.
- Resolving a conflict by choosing one side without understanding both changes.
- Rewriting history that other developers have already based work on.
- Using force push on a shared protected branch.

## Key terms

- commit — A recorded set of related file changes with a message explaining the change.
- rebase — Moving local commits onto a newer base to create a different history.
- merge — Combining two development histories while preserving both lines of work.
- revert — Creating a new commit that safely undoes an earlier shared change.

topics.name: Business Workflow Automation
topics.description: Business workflow automation lets software perform a repeatable sequence of work that people currently do by hand. A safe automation keeps exceptional or uncertain decisions visible to a person.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Business workflow automation lets software perform repeatable steps that people previously completed by hand. Deterministic work can run automatically, while uncertain, exceptional, or high-impact decisions remain visible to a person.

## What problem does it solve?

Manual copying, checking, and notification work is slow and easy to perform inconsistently. A rushed automation can make the problem worse by repeating side effects, hiding failures, or applying the same rule to cases that actually need human judgment.

## How it works

An operator copies a delayed shipment into a partner portal and then sends a customer update. The automation validates the shipment, submits it once with a stable operation key, records the result, and creates a review task when the partner rejects the request.

- Map the current workflow, including inputs, owners, exceptions, and the reason for each step.
- Automate deterministic steps and keep judgment-heavy or high-impact decisions behind human approval.
- Make external actions safe to retry, visible in logs, and recoverable after partial failure.
- Measure time saved, error reduction, and unresolved cases while keeping a manual fallback.

## Common mistakes

- Automating an unclear or broken process before understanding why each step exists.
- Treating every failed step as safe to retry, which can create duplicate external actions.
- Reporting only successful runs and hiding the cases that still require manual repair.

## Key terms

- workflow — A sequence of steps and decisions used to complete a business task.
- idempotent step — A workflow action that can be retried without creating an additional side effect.
- human review — A point where a person checks or approves an uncertain or high-impact decision.
- audit trail — A record of what the automation attempted, decided, and produced.

topics.name: TypeScript Generics
topics.description: A TypeScript generic lets one function, type, or component work with several value types while preserving the relationship between its inputs and outputs.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

A TypeScript generic is a typed abstraction with a placeholder for a concrete type supplied or inferred when the code is used. It lets reusable functions, types, and components preserve meaningful relationships between their inputs and outputs.

## What problem does it solve?

Without generics, reusable code often returns any or repeats nearly identical types for strings, shipments, users, and other values. Any removes safety, while duplicated versions drift apart. A generic preserves useful type information without knowing the concrete type in advance.

## How it works

An API result type contains data and an error. ApiResult<Shipment> makes data a Shipment, while ApiResult<User> makes it a User. The same result structure is reused, and each caller still receives the correct data type.

- Declare a type parameter that represents a value type chosen later.
- Use that parameter where input and output types must remain connected.
- Add a constraint only when the implementation requires a particular capability.
- Prefer a concrete type when reuse or a meaningful type relationship does not exist.

## Common mistakes

- Using a generic when one concrete type would be easier to read.
- Adding several type parameters that callers cannot understand or infer.
- Using a generic only to hide an unsafe cast inside the implementation.

## Key terms

- type parameter — A placeholder type that is supplied or inferred when the generic code is used.
- generic constraint — A rule that limits which types may be supplied to a generic.
- type inference — The compiler determines a generic type from the values passed by the caller.

topics.name: React Form State
topics.description: React form state represents what the user entered, which fields are invalid or changed, and what is happening while the application submits the form.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

React form state represents user input, validation feedback, changed fields, and submission progress. A clear state model keeps the displayed values and errors consistent while preserving input through failed or uncertain submissions.

## What problem does it solve?

A complex form can lose edits, show errors too early, submit twice, or overwrite server feedback when every field manages itself differently. Client checks improve the experience, but the backend must still validate the final request because browser data can be changed.

## How it works

A transport-order form tracks values, touched fields, and whether the user changed anything. It shows local format errors, disables a second submission while saving, and maps backend field errors back to the matching inputs.

- Choose one clear owner for the form values and submission state.
- Separate field validation from business rules that only the backend can decide.
- Show errors after a useful user action, such as leaving a field or submitting.
- Preserve user input and present a clear retry path when submission fails.

## Common mistakes

- Treating browser validation as the final security or business-rule check.
- Using one loading boolean for initial data loading and form submission.
- Clearing the form after a timeout when the final server result is still unknown.

## Key terms

- controlled field — A form field whose displayed value comes from React state.
- touched field — A field the user has already interacted with, often used to decide when to show an error.
- dirty state — A signal that the current form values differ from their original values.

topics.name: Asynchronous Error Handling
topics.description: Asynchronous error handling makes failures from promises and awaited operations follow a clear path to logging, cleanup, retry decisions, and a safe response.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Asynchronous error handling gives every failed promise or awaited operation an intentional path. That path may add context, choose a recovery action, release resources, log the cause, or translate the failure into a consistent response.

## What problem does it solve?

Network, database, and background operations fail after they have started. A missing await, an unhandled rejection, or Promise.all with the wrong failure expectation can skip cleanup, hide the original cause, or return success before the work is complete.

## How it works

An order endpoint awaits validation and a database write inside one error boundary. Known validation conflicts become a clear client response, unexpected failures are logged with a correlation id, and the HTTP handler passes the error to one central mapper.

- Await or return every promise whose result belongs to the current operation.
- Catch errors at a boundary that can add context or choose a recovery action.
- Classify expected errors separately from unexpected programming or infrastructure failures.
- Use finally or cancellation to release resources and stop work that is no longer needed.

## Common mistakes

- Starting a promise without awaiting, returning, or deliberately monitoring it.
- Catching an error only to replace it with a generic message and lose the original cause.
- Assuming Promise.all continues successfully when one required operation rejects.

## Key terms

- rejection — The failed state of a promise.
- error boundary — A deliberate place where an error is translated, logged, retried, or returned.
- finally — A block that runs after success or failure and is commonly used for cleanup.

topics.name: Technical Prototyping
topics.description: A technical prototype is a small experiment built to answer a risky technical or product question before the team commits to a production solution.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

A technical prototype is a small, time-limited experiment built to answer an important technical or product uncertainty. Its purpose is to replace an assumption with evidence before the team commits to a production design.

## What problem does it solve?

A team can spend weeks integrating a new library or artificial-intelligence (AI) service before learning that its quality, latency, security, or cost does not fit the real workflow. A prototype reduces this uncertainty, but it becomes dangerous when temporary shortcuts silently turn into production architecture.

## How it works

Before adding an AI shipment assistant, the team tests twenty representative cases with masked data. The prototype measures correct recommendations, harmful failures, response time, and cost, then records which production controls would still be required.

- Write the specific assumption or risk the experiment must test.
- Use representative data and define success and stop criteria before building.
- Measure the result, including failure cases, cost, security, and operational effort.
- Decide explicitly whether to discard, extend, or rebuild the prototype for production.

## Common mistakes

- Building a broad demo without one clear question it must answer.
- Testing only successful examples that do not represent real production input.
- Shipping prototype code whose shortcuts, credentials, and missing controls were never reviewed.

## Key terms

- technical spike — A time-limited experiment used to investigate a technical uncertainty.
- success criterion — A measurable condition that tells the team whether the experiment answered its question positively.
- throwaway prototype — An experiment intended to be discarded rather than evolved directly into production code.

topics.name: React Performance Optimization
topics.description: React performance optimization reduces user-visible delay by removing proven expensive rendering work. It starts with measurement and changes only the components or data flows that create a real bottleneck.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

React performance optimization begins by measuring a slow user interaction or expensive render. The team then reduces the specific proven cost through better state boundaries, stable inputs, memoization, batching, or list virtualization.

## What problem does it solve?

A page may become slow because too many components rerender, a calculation repeats, or a large list creates more browser work than the user can see. Adding memoization everywhere increases complexity and may add overhead without fixing the real cause.

## How it works

A live shipment table becomes slow during updates. React Profiler shows that every row rerenders and thousands of hidden rows remain mounted. Stable row inputs and list virtualization reduce the work to changed and visible rows.

- Measure the slow user interaction with React Profiler and browser tools.
- Find which render, calculation, list, or update frequency creates the cost.
- Reduce that cost with better state boundaries, stable values, memoization, batching, or virtualization.
- Measure again to confirm that the change improved the user experience.

## Common mistakes

- Adding React.memo, useMemo, and useCallback everywhere without measuring.
- Optimizing a cheap component while a large list or network waterfall causes the delay.
- Measuring only development mode, where React may deliberately perform extra work.

## Key terms

- profiling — Measuring where an application spends time during a real interaction.
- memoization — Reusing a previous result when the relevant inputs have not changed.
- virtualization — Rendering only the visible part of a large collection.

topics.name: Application Scalability
topics.description: Application scalability means increasing the amount of useful work a system can handle without allowing response time, failures, or operating cost to grow uncontrollably.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Application scalability is the ability to handle more useful work while keeping response time, failures, and operating cost under control. Scaling requires identifying the resource that currently limits throughput before adding capacity or partitioning work.

## What problem does it solve?

A service that works for a small workload may become slow or unstable when requests, records, or background jobs grow. Adding more servers does not help when the real limit is a database query, shared lock, partner API, or unbounded queue.

## How it works

A shipment-search API receives ten times more traffic. Metrics show that the application servers are healthy, but one database query is saturated. The team improves the query and index, adds pagination, then increases stateless API instances only after the data bottleneck is removed.

- Define the expected workload and the response-time and error targets that must remain acceptable.
- Measure which resource or dependency becomes saturated first.
- Reduce unnecessary work and remove the current bottleneck.
- Add capacity or partition work where the architecture can safely scale.
- Load-test again and check reliability and cost at the new level.

## Common mistakes

- Adding application servers before measuring whether the application layer is the bottleneck.
- Using average traffic while ignoring bursts, hot data, or one expensive request type.
- Calling a design scalable without testing its database, dependency, and failure limits.

## Key terms

- bottleneck — The component or resource that currently limits the system's total useful throughput.
- horizontal scaling — Adding more service instances and distributing work between them.
- saturation — The point where a resource is fully occupied and additional work must wait or fail.

topics.name: API Authorization
topics.description: API authorization decides whether an authenticated caller may perform a particular action on a particular resource. The decision must be enforced by the backend, not only represented in the user interface.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Application programming interface (API) authorization decides whether an authenticated caller may perform a specific action on a specific resource. The backend must enforce that decision using ownership, tenant, role, or policy data rather than trusting controls shown in the user interface.

## What problem does it solve?

A valid login does not mean that every user may read or change every record. If the backend checks only roles or trusts hidden frontend controls, one authenticated user may access another tenant's shipment or perform an operation outside their responsibility.

## How it works

An employee sends a valid token with an order update. The API verifies that the employee belongs to the order's tenant and has permission to edit that order before applying the change.

- Authenticate the caller and identify the requested action and resource.
- Load or derive the ownership, tenant, role, or policy information needed for the decision.
- Allow or reject the operation on the backend before the protected action runs.
- Log denied high-risk operations without exposing sensitive policy details.

## Common mistakes

- Using hidden or disabled frontend controls as the authorization boundary.
- Checking a broad role but not the caller's access to the specific resource.
- Trusting a tenant or user identifier supplied by the client without comparing it with server-side identity data.

## Key terms

- permission — A rule that allows a caller to perform a particular action.
- resource-level check — A permission decision for the exact object being requested or changed.
- tenant isolation — Preventing one customer's users and data from being accessed by another customer.

topics.name: REST versus GraphQL
topics.description: REST and GraphQL are two ways to expose application data through an API. The better choice depends on client needs, domain boundaries, caching, security, and operational complexity.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

REST exposes predefined resource endpoints, while GraphQL provides a typed query language in which clients select the fields they need. The better choice depends on client data shapes, domain boundaries, caching, authorization, query cost, observability, and team experience.

## What problem does it solve?

REST can require several requests or return fields a screen does not need, while GraphQL adds a schema, resolver layer, query-cost controls, and different caching concerns. Choosing only from fashion can move complexity without improving the product.

## How it works

A dashboard needs shipment, customer, and pricing data in one screen. GraphQL can combine those fields through one query, while a simple order-update workflow may remain clearer as a REST endpoint with a fixed request and response.

- List the clients, data shapes, write operations, and change patterns the API must support.
- Compare REST's explicit endpoints with GraphQL's client-selected typed queries.
- Consider caching, authorization, query cost, observability, and team experience.
- Choose the simpler option that fits the dominant workflows, and keep boundaries consistent.

## Common mistakes

- Assuming one GraphQL request always means less backend or database work.
- Creating many frontend-specific REST endpoints without a stable domain boundary.
- Adopting both styles without clear ownership, monitoring, or a reason for the added complexity.

## Key terms

- resolver — A function that provides the value for a field requested through a GraphQL schema.
- over-fetching — Returning more data than a client needs for its current task.
- under-fetching — Requiring additional requests because one response does not contain enough data.

topics.name: TypeScript Object Type Composition
topics.description: TypeScript object type composition creates related object shapes from clear building blocks instead of copying fields or forcing every application layer to share one oversized type.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

TypeScript object type composition builds related object shapes from smaller meaningful types. It can reuse real relationships through interface extension, intersections, and utility types without forcing database records, API messages, and forms to share one oversized model.

## What problem does it solve?

A database record, API request, API response, and editable form often contain similar fields but do not mean the same thing. Copying every type causes drift, while reusing one giant interface can expose private fields and make optional values appear valid everywhere.

## How it works

A Shipment type represents the stored domain object. A create request uses Pick for required user-provided fields, while an update command defines exactly which fields may change instead of using Partial<Shipment> for every property.

- Name the purpose and boundary of each object type before deciding what it should share.
- Use interface extension, intersections, or small reusable types when the relationship is real.
- Use Pick, Omit, Readonly, or another utility type to derive a precise shape.
- Validate external objects at runtime even when their TypeScript type is derived correctly.

## Common mistakes

- Using one domain or database type for every API, form, and persistence boundary.
- Using Partial on an entire object when only a few fields are valid updates.
- Creating deep chains of derived types that make the final shape difficult to understand.

## Key terms

- type composition — Creating a type by combining or selecting parts of other types.
- utility type — A built-in TypeScript type such as Pick, Omit, Partial, or Readonly that transforms another type.
- intersection — A type that must satisfy all object types joined with the ampersand operator.
