questions.prompt: Describe a feature you delivered across frontend and backend. How did you clarify the need, make technical decisions, release it, and determine whether it worked?
questions.answerStructureHint: Give one concrete example: problem and ownership, key decisions and trade-offs, collaboration, safe delivery, and measurable result or lesson.

expectedPoints.label: Defines a concrete problem and personal ownership
expectedPoints.description: The answer explains the users, business need, constraints, and exactly which decisions or implementation parts the candidate owned.

expectedPoints.label: Explains decisions instead of listing technologies
expectedPoints.description: The answer describes at least one meaningful trade-off, why the chosen design fit the context, and how frontend and backend contracts were coordinated.

expectedPoints.label: Connects delivery to evidence and learning
expectedPoints.description: The answer covers testing and rollout and gives a measurable result, user feedback, production signal, or honest lesson.

followUps.prompt: What alternative did you reject, and what would have made that alternative the better choice?
Tell me about a disagreement with a product or business stakeholder during that feature. How did you reach a decision without losing the underlying business goal?

questions.title: Automating a repetitive business workflow
questions.prompt: Describe a repetitive business process you automated. How did you understand the existing work, decide what should remain manual, handle failures, and prove that the automation was useful?
questions.answerStructureHint: Use one concrete example: the manual problem, the selected automation boundary, reliability and human fallback, then measurable outcome and lessons.

expectedPoints.label: Understands the real workflow before automating it
expectedPoints.description: The answer identifies the users, repeated steps, inputs, exceptions, ownership, and the business reason for the process instead of starting with a tool.

expectedPoints.label: Chooses a safe automation boundary
expectedPoints.description: The answer automates deterministic work, keeps uncertain decisions visible to a person, and designs retries, idempotency, logging, and recovery for partial failures.

expectedPoints.label: Measures value and operational quality
expectedPoints.description: The answer uses evidence such as time saved, reduced errors, adoption, failed-run rate, or support feedback and mentions what was changed after real use.

followUps.prompt: Which part of the process did you deliberately leave manual, and what risk or uncertainty made that the better choice?

questions.title: Evaluating a new technology with a prototype
questions.prompt: Redspher wants to explore a new AI or automation technology for a core logistics workflow. How would you design a prototype that produces a useful production decision rather than only an impressive demo?
questions.answerStructureHint: State the uncertainty, choose representative cases, define success and stop criteria, measure quality and operational constraints, then decide what happens to the prototype.

expectedPoints.label: Defines the uncertainty the prototype must resolve
expectedPoints.description: The answer starts with a specific technical or business assumption and a bounded workflow instead of building a general demonstration.

expectedPoints.label: Tests with representative evidence and criteria
expectedPoints.description: The answer uses realistic cases and defines quality, latency, security, cost, integration, and failure thresholds before evaluating results.

expectedPoints.label: Makes an explicit production decision
expectedPoints.description: The answer records findings and decides whether to stop, continue, or rebuild, without silently treating prototype shortcuts as production architecture.

followUps.prompt: Which result would make you stop the prototype even if the demo looked technically impressive?

questions.title: Adding a safe logistics assistant
questions.prompt: How would you add an LLM assistant that summarizes shipment problems and suggests actions without letting unreliable output directly change business data?
questions.answerStructureHint: Limit authority, ground the answer, validate structured output, check permissions, evaluate quality, and keep approval and audit paths.

expectedPoints.label: Starts with limited model authority
expectedPoints.description: The answer uses the model for summaries, drafts, or recommendations and separates suggestion from execution.

expectedPoints.label: Validates every proposed action
expectedPoints.description: The answer requires structured output and checks its schema, permission, allowed action, and business rules in normal code.

expectedPoints.label: Grounds and evaluates the feature
expectedPoints.description: The answer uses authorized source data and measures realistic quality, harmful failures, latency, cost, and operator feedback.

followUps.prompt: What deterministic checks would run before an AI-proposed customer message is sent?

questions.title: Explaining an AI or LLM project you built
questions.prompt: Describe an AI, LLM, agent, or automation prototype you built or explored. What problem did it address, how did the solution work, and what limitations did you discover?
questions.answerStructureHint: Use one concrete project: problem and personal contribution, architecture and data flow, evaluation and safeguards, then limitations and what you would improve.

expectedPoints.label: Explains a concrete problem and personal contribution
expectedPoints.description: The answer identifies the user or workflow, why AI or automation was considered useful, and which design and implementation work the candidate personally performed.

expectedPoints.label: Explains the real architecture and control boundaries
expectedPoints.description: The answer describes the model or API, application orchestration, input and output handling, and which decisions remained in deterministic code or human control.

expectedPoints.label: Discusses evaluation, limitations, and lessons honestly
expectedPoints.description: The answer mentions realistic testing and at least one limitation involving quality, hallucination, latency, cost, privacy, security, or operational reliability, plus a concrete improvement.

followUps.prompt: What failed or surprised you during the prototype, and how did that change the design?

questions.title: Agent-assisted shipment exception handling
questions.prompt: Design an agent-assisted workflow for a delayed shipment that gathers context and proposes a response while keeping the operator in control.
questions.answerStructureHint: Start with deterministic detection, define narrow tools, manage state and budgets, require approval, and capture the outcome.

expectedPoints.label: Uses deterministic logic to start the workflow
expectedPoints.description: The answer detects known delay conditions with rules or events instead of asking the model to discover every exception.

expectedPoints.label: Uses narrow authorized tools with hard limits
expectedPoints.description: The answer retrieves data and performs actions through typed capabilities with permission checks, step limits, timeouts, and cost limits.

expectedPoints.label: Keeps high-impact decisions with the operator
expectedPoints.description: The answer presents explained options, requires approval where needed, and records the selected action and result.

followUps.prompt: Which low-risk action might be automated, and which action should still require approval?

questions.title: Understanding and securing MCP
questions.prompt: What problem does the Model Context Protocol solve, and what security controls would you require for an MCP server that exposes shipment tools?
questions.answerStructureHint: Explain discovery and calling, host-client-server roles, identity, per-resource authorization, validation, limits, and auditability.

expectedPoints.label: Explains the interoperability goal
expectedPoints.description: The answer describes a standard way for AI hosts to discover and use tools or context from separate servers.

expectedPoints.label: Authenticates and authorizes every tool call
expectedPoints.description: The answer verifies caller identity and permission for the specific shipment or order instead of trusting the model.

expectedPoints.label: Validates, limits, and audits the capability
expectedPoints.description: The answer uses typed schemas, narrow actions, timeouts, rate limits, sensitive-data controls, and an audit trail.

followUps.prompt: Why does exposing a function through MCP not make that function safe automatically?

questions.title: Integrating with a legacy PHP system
questions.prompt: A new React and Node.js feature must use data and behavior from a legacy PHP application. How would you integrate it without making the new code depend on legacy internals?
questions.answerStructureHint: Learn the real behavior, define ownership, create a stable facade or adapter, protect it with tests, and migrate incrementally.

expectedPoints.label: Learns actual behavior before redesigning it
expectedPoints.description: The answer uses code, logs, production examples, data ownership, and user knowledge to find undocumented rules.

expectedPoints.label: Places a stable boundary around the legacy system
expectedPoints.description: The answer uses a facade, API, or anti-corruption adapter instead of direct database and internal-code coupling.

expectedPoints.label: Protects behavior and changes it incrementally
expectedPoints.description: The answer adds characterization and contract tests, monitoring, feature flags, comparison, and rollback.

followUps.prompt: Why is directly reading and writing the legacy database from the new Node.js service risky?
